preFix.title: Maintaining and lubricating the mains stopcock
preFix.subtitle: How to find, test, and free a seized stopcock before a leak makes it urgent
preFix.excerpt: A seized stopcock that cannot be turned in an emergency causes water damage that takes days to dry out. Stopcocks seize because the gland packing and brass valve body corrode when unused for years. A 10-minute annual check and occasional re-lubrication keeps them operational. This covers location, testing, lubrication, and when to replace.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIPHE (Chartered Institute of Plumbing) best practice guidance. Water UK consumer guidance on stopcocks. Builderscrack/Which? plumbing maintenance guides.

content.text: The internal mains stopcock is the single most important valve in the house in a water emergency. A burst pipe or failed appliance valve can discharge 400 to 600 litres per hour. The difference between 5 minutes to shut off and 30 minutes to find a plumber is the difference between a mop and a replacement floor. Testing and lubricating the stopcock takes 10 minutes and costs nothing.
Standard location: under the kitchen sink, on the cold supply pipe where it enters the house from the external wall or floor. It is usually a brass valve with a flat-head or crosshead spindle.

Alternative locations: utility room, airing cupboard, under the stairs, or in a ground-floor toilet cupboard. In flats, it may be inside the flat (check the kitchen cupboard near the external wall) or in a shared riser cupboard on the landing.

External boundary stopcock: there is also a stopcock in the pavement outside the property boundary (water company owned, operated with a long key). This is the backup shutoff if the internal one fails completely.
Lubricating a stiff or seized stopcock
Apply a small amount of petroleum jelly (Vaseline) or silicone grease around the spindle where it enters the valve body. Do not use WD-40 or mineral oil, which are not safe for potable water systems.

Use an adjustable spanner or pliers to turn the spindle slowly if it will not move by hand. Apply light force only; do not wrench it. If it snaps, the house is without water until a plumber installs a new valve.
nut is accessible (a hexagonal nut around the spindle above the valve body), slackening it by a quarter turn sometimes frees a seized spindle. Retighten after turning.
After freeing: turn the valve fully off and fully on three to five times to work the lubricant through the mechanism. Leave in the open (fully anticlockwise) position.

Brass stopcocks last 20 to 40 years in normal service. Replace when: the valve leaks from the gland even after retightening; the spindle head is stripped and cannot be operated; the body shows pinhole corrosion; or the valve has seized completely and cannot be freed without excessive force. The work requires shutting off at the external boundary stopcock. Either do this yourself or have a plumber do the replacement.
